calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jun-12-2026 Continued To Oct-12-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Two, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities, Separate Statement, Declaration Of Eric Williams
Off Calendar May-19-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT JAMES LUNG's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Two.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant James Lung's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Two is GRANTED.

At issue is Plaintiff Keir Kramlich's response to Special Rogs., Set Two, no. 11. Defendant's motion is based on Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.300. "On receipt of a response to interrogatories, the propounding party may move for an order compelling a further response if the propounding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) An answer to a particular interrogatory is evasive or incomplete. (2) An exercise of the option to produce documents under Section 2030.230 is unwarranted or the required specification of those documents is inadequate. (3) An objection to an interrogatory is without merit or too general." (Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.300(a).)

Defendant demonstrated adequate meet and confer. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2016.040.)

The discovery at issue appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (See Code of Civil Procedure Section 2017.010.) Defendant demonstrated Plaintiff's response was incomplete and/or evasive. As to the response identified below, Plaintiff did not demonstrate the merit of any objection. The objections, thus, are overruled.

As to Special Rog no. 11, the court expressly overrules Plaintiff's privacy objection. Where an objecting party establishes that a discovery response would result in "an obvious invasion of an interest fundamental to personal autonomy," the requesting party must establish a compelling interest justifying the request to overcome a privacy objection. (See Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 556.) Otherwise, a privacy objection generally triggers a three-step analysis: "The party asserting a privacy right must establish a legally protected privacy interest, an objectively reasonable expectation of privacy in the given circumstances, and a threatened intrusion that is serious. The party seeking information may raise in response whatever legitimate and important countervailing interests disclosure serves, while the party seeking protection may identify feasible alternatives that serve the same interests or protective measures that would diminish the loss of privacy. A court must then balance these competing considerations." (Id. at p. 552; see also Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Ass'n (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1; County of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (2021) 65 Cal.App.5th 621, 639; see also Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court (1975) 15 Cal. 3d 652, 657 [court must "carefully balance" claimed right to privacy against public interest in obtaining just results in litigation].) The court finds Plaintiff has a general privacy interest in the information but here Plaintiff has put the information at issue by his claims and damages demands. Defendant has a strong interest in complete responses for many reasons, including establishing a baseline and developing the record re causation. Defendant's interest predominate as to the information requested in Special Rog no. 11.. Plaintiff did not identify a feasible alternative that serve the same interests or protective measures that would diminish the loss of privacy. In any event, a protective order can preserve privacy interests to a large extent.

Plaintiff to provide further response to Defendant's Special Rogs., Set Two, no. 11no later than 14 days from this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 302 a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
